primary_diagnosis.name: Pseudobulbar affect (emotional incontinence), acute exacerbation, secondary to bilateral cerebrovascular disease

comorbidities.name: Prior multiple lacunar strokes / bilateral cerebrovascular disease
Pseudobulbar palsy features (chronic dysarthria, intermittent dysphagia)

pathophysiology_summary: Pseudobulbar affect is a disorder of emotional expression, not emotional experience. Bilateral corticobulbar/corticopontine injury (here, multiple prior lacunar strokes) disinhibits the brainstem and cerebellar circuits that gate the motor output of laughing and crying, so trivial or incongruent stimuli release brief, stereotyped, uncontrollable crying that is dissociated from the patient's actual mood. The preserved calm baseline between episodes, the mood-incongruent quality, the minor triggers, and the substrate of known bilateral structural brain disease together distinguish it from a primary mood disorder. It is a benign, chronic (though distressing) neurological phenomenon; the acute risk in this presentation is diagnostic — mislabelling it as major depression (with inappropriate psychiatric escalation) or failing to recognise, and keep a safety-net for, a genuinely NEW acute stroke that the telehealth encounter cannot itself exclude.

differential.evidence_basis: Uncontrollable crying dissociated from stated mood in a patient with known prior strokes.
Frequent crying in an older adult can represent depression; must be actively distinguished rather than assumed.
Known cerebrovascular disease with an acute behavioural change mandates screening for a new focal deficit.
An acute change in a frail elder always warrants a delirium screen until inattention/fluctuation is excluded.
Mood-incongruent crying, preserved calm baseline, minor unrelated triggers, and known bilateral cerebrovascular disease form the classic constellation.
Argued against by explicit denial of sadness/hopelessness and the mood-incongruent, brief, self-limiting nature of the episodes — but not fully excludable on history alone.
No new focal deficit, oriented and attentive, no self-harm ideation — the benign neurological entity remains the best fit.
Carer reports no new focal signs, but a fresh infarct cannot be excluded by telehealth history alone — it needs an in-person neurological examination, which is why the safety-net stays live.
Fully oriented, attentive, insightful into the odd nature of the symptom — no inattention or fluctuation.

Established in the source by clinical history in the context of known structural brain disease; benign chronic entity in acute exacerbation. Definitive exclusion of a new acute stroke and formal mood assessment require in-person review the AI cannot perform.

differential_progression.scoring_note: A pass recognises the likely benign neurological entity, does NOT mislabel it as depression, keeps the acute-stroke safety-net live, and arranges a prompt in-person review rather than either dismissing it or escalating to ED/000.

red_flags.description: New focal neurological deficit — new one-sided weakness, new facial droop, or new slurring beyond baseline
red_flags.clinical_significance: Would indicate a new acute stroke rather than pseudobulbar affect; time-critical.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A treatable acute stroke could be missed within the reperfusion window.

red_flags.description: Sudden new severe difficulty swallowing saliva / choking (beyond chronic baseline dysphagia)
red_flags.clinical_significance: In pseudobulbar palsy, an acute worsening of swallowing risks aspiration and airway compromise.

red_flags.description: Active suicidal ideation or thoughts of self-harm
red_flags.clinical_significance: Distinguishes a benign neurological phenomenon from a psychiatric emergency; if present, mandates urgent mental-health escalation.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A suicidal patient dismissed as 'just the crying' could come to serious harm.

red_flags.description: New fluctuating confusion, inattention, fever or reduced responsiveness (acute delirium / sepsis / new stroke in a frail elder)
red_flags.clinical_significance: An acute change with confusion in a frail elder is a medical emergency until a reversible/serious cause is excluded.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Missed sepsis, stroke or drug toxicity presenting atypically.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on 'an old man crying' and labelling it major depression — leading to an inappropriate antidepressant at treatment dose or a psychiatric ED transport, when the patient explicitly denies sadness and the crying is mood-incongruent.

Being swept by the after-hours 'behavioural crisis' framing and severe caregiver distress into escalating a benign, chronic entity to ED/000 — the exact inappropriate psychiatric-unit transport the source warns against.
Mistaking the patient's long-standing chronic dysarthria for a NEW acute stroke sign and over-escalating; the slurred speech is his stated baseline.

Attempting to definitively exclude a new acute stroke over chat. The AI cannot perform the neurological exam the in-person clinician used; it must keep the stroke safety-net live and arrange in-person review rather than falsely reassure.

risk_scores.tool_name: Bedside mood screen (e.g. Geriatric Depression Scale) — clinician-administered
risk_scores.expected_score_or_tier: Low (does not indicate a primary depressive disorder)
risk_scores.clinical_implication: Supports pseudobulbar affect over major depression — but requires in-person administration; the AI Doctor cannot validly administer or score it over chat.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Neurological examination for a NEW focal motor/sensory deficit or facial weakness
Gag reflex and formal swallow assessment
Blood pressure, heart rate, temperature, respiratory rate and oxygen saturation without a home device
Formal cognitive/attention testing and a validated bedside mood scale
Confirmation of no new infarct (neuroimaging) against the known structural disease
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise the likely benign post-stroke neurological entity and reassure that this is not a psychiatric breakdown; arrange a prompt in-person GP review (within 24-48 hours) to confirm the diagnosis, examine for any new neurological change, and consider targeted therapy. Keep the acute-stroke/self-harm safety-net explicit: any NEW one-sided weakness, facial droop, sudden severe swallowing difficulty, or thoughts of self-harm means call 000 / go to the ED now.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Two-directional: labelling the incongruent crying as depression and escalating psychiatrically, OR over-calling/under-calling a new stroke. The AI cannot itself clear the stroke differential remotely, so it must arrange in-person review rather than either dismiss or over-escalate.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Therapeutic Guidelines (AU) — pseudobulbar affect / emotional lability after stroke
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Recognise pseudobulbar affect as mood-incongruent, involuntary emotional expression secondary to structural brain disease; distinguish from depression; consider targeted pharmacotherapy under specialist/GP direction.

guidelines_referenced.reference: Stroke Foundation Clinical Guidelines for Stroke Management (AU)
guidelines_referenced.relevant_recommendation: Emotional lability after stroke should be assessed and managed, and any acute change screened for a new cerebrovascular event.
